speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the middle of a story. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were talking about being in Theresienstadt and being on the protective list of Jacob Edelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had said you hadn't-- he hadn't put you on it yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe it was done right at that point. And my father would not consider doing that. He said it wasn't the right thing to do, to have-- because the Germans demanded certain numbers. And that would mean that somebody else would have to take his place. And he didn't want that on his conscience. He wouldn't do it. So we told Edelstein that we were about to break up the family and that we'd all stay, or the children would go. My mother refused to leave my father behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came and he said, you're forcing me to tell you a secret. I know you won't be talking about it. You have to stay behind. This is the way it's done.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are transports arriving during the night who incorporated into this immediately. And especially from Germany, the old people that come, they don't survive. Many of them are dead on arrival, and their names are on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we're doing is we're-- I'm going to substitute one of these dead people's names for yours. And you're not going to take-- nobody will take your place. These people are not around anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are never-- the lists are never complete because there are always people passing away during the night. And this is what I'm going to do. And that was acceptable to my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- we stayed behind. What happened was that if you were on somebody's protective list, that when that person fell out of favor, you went along with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- what we had saved, a few months of going east. It was well known the transports to the east went to Auschwitz. And because I was in this [NON-ENGLISH], which went right along through my you know, internment years--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the younger people who were helping?

speaker: interviewee
text: --who were helping and who were called in to-- to fill in, to-- to keep order and to clean up or whatever was necessary. So in-- in the fall of '43, Jacob Edelstein was called. And that he-- his time has come. They're through with him. He and his family and all people attached to him will go on the next transfer to Auschwitz, which happened. It was quite an experience. These-- these cattle wagons, which had just a little-- two little windows high up. It took a long time. And again, my father was-- did not to lose his faith. He gave us courage and explained and said that these things can strengthen us too. They don't have to turn us against God. And we have to believe that things will turn out all right. And it was quite a horrible trip. And as you know, probably, these-- a crisis like that brings out the best and the worst in people. Some were wonderful and decent, and others turned into thoughtless and very, very selfish people. Can't judge them for that either. We arrived in Auschwitz, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long do you remember the trip taking?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seemed endless. I really don't know. It was more than 24 hours. And there were the Germans and the well-known sign, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And-- and there was Poland. And there was-- there were the dogs and the SS with their highly-polished boots and their uniforms, you know, belting out their instructions. And we were put into a camp together, which was very unusual, because women were always separated from the men. And all kind of procedures, selections were made, and all that. None of that happened to us. That's why I always say you can't really say the story is the same. It's so different for so many people. And we arrived there. We were all in one enclosure. There was barbed wire, which was electrified, we were told. If you touch it, you're gone. And one side of the barracks were the men. And on the other side were the women. And the next day, they separated us from the children. But on the-- on the day we arrived, they tattooed our arms. And I remember my mother giving us instructions. My sister was very tall for her age and developed. And I was just about the right level. I was a child. And she said my sister was to go ahead and not look back if she was told to do something, because she thought maybe they wouldn't let me through because I'm so little. And she was going to-- and I was going to be in the middle. And she was going to walk behind me. And wherever I went, she would go with me. And my sister was to go ahead. That was the way it was going to be. And I had an exquisite little number on my arm. That girl that did it was a Polish prisoner, a political prisoner. She had a round red circle that means she was a prisoner for political reasons. And she tattooed this little number on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said to her, how can you do this to a child? You're sitting here doing this. And she said, don't worry. She's going to grow up and get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband will buy her a bracelet, a diamond bracelet that will cover it. I never forget that. Some of these silly things you never forget. And-- but we all were let through, the children and everybody. Soon enough, this SS man made a speech to us. And he said that we are a [NON-ENGLISH]. We are show camp. And all the dignitaries that were going to come from everywhere, they were going to show him our camp. And that they are-- the children are going to be entertained during the day. And there were going to be all kinds of tasks for the-- for the men, and that we are to behave ourselves and to follow instructions, and nothing will happen to us. And that was already very different again, because they didn't shave our hair, the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you realized at that point--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we saw the next--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that the treatment was different?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw-- we had-- had reports. And we saw the-- the women in the next enclosure who were speaking Hungarian. And they were-- it-- it was beyond description what you saw there. And we-- all our clothes was taken from us with the goodies in it, the coins and whatever to save our lives. We were-- they-- the explanation for it was that they had to de-louse us because we are lousy Jews. We're all full of stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave us clothing. Everybody was assigned a little pile. And everything was taken away that we had worn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very different. We were allowed a-- a message to write with our tattooed number on it as a reference number, 30 words in capital letters, to whoever we wanted to write to. Now, because of the [NON-ENGLISH], because of my organization, we had contact points. There was a-- one half-Jew who belonged to our group. His father was a-- a Gentile. The mother was Jewish. And he was an engineer. And he helped them-- helped the Germans repair the bridges as they were bombed. So as long as they needed him, they were going to keep him there. We wrote to him address-- there were all-- you'd be amazed what you can put into 30 words. I wrote that [NON-ENGLISH] is with us. That means empty stomach is always with us. And then there was a-- one of ours-- our group that had been killed in a car accident. In our time, it was very shattering experience. We said this person, Joe such and such, is always near us, to let them know that the things are very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like in a code.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all in a code. And they even sent a package once, to my name. Everything was cut up, and most of it was taken out, because they thought there were weapons maybe inside that loaf of bread or whatever. But we-- when I was called, my family was just so scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- why are they calling me? I'm just the youngest of the family. And the reason was that they handed over this-- this wreck of a package that had-- but it was a tremendous lift for me to have contact right from my home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all around us, people were arriving by the thousands. And pitiful groups, some of them, who-- I think we were a little better off because we knew where we were going. And they didn't know. And if the ovens were available, they were gassing them as they arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you knew what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew exactly what was going on. We were briefed. There were certain things that I knew that I didn't tell my parents. I didn't want them to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: By who-- who were you briefed by?

speaker: interviewee
text: By madrichim, by our leaders who had contacts with the outside. And so we knew what was going on. You could smell it. Mother would say to us, children, the ovens are all active. They-- you could smell it. Chimneys were smoking. And you could hear them coming in, the transports at night. And those that were tattooed were the privileged ones, because that meant that you weren't going straight into-- to your death. You-- you had a little time, and just wait for it to happen. And that didn't mean that you didn't-- that you weren't sent. It was-- just meant that not immediately. Either the ovens were overworked, or they were going to pause for another reason for a day or so. But it went constantly. In the meantime, our camp was organized. The men on one side, the women on the other side. And every day, we could see the men. We saw our father for about an hour every day. And he was a very disciplined man, lean. And he-- he knew how to fast. And he was very-- I knew that if-- unless they killed him, he wouldn't just die of starvation or-- or because his morale was crushed, which the men-- men fell to very easily. For some reason-- it's a study I should make one day-- the women handled it emotionally better than the men. And-- but the job they had given my father in the beginning was to carry stones. It was the most idiotic work. It was a pile of stones which they carried from one place up the hill. And two days later, they'd carry it from up there down back there again. This is how they occupied the men. Maybe because men would have done something. Maybe there would've been a revolution or some kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: To keep them busy.

speaker: interviewee
text: --keep them busy. And my father's beautiful hands were bleeding from those stones. And, you know, one of those little cushions that you have, that you do something for someone. They didn't have a jacket for me. And so they gave me two knitted things to wear, on-- one on top of the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took that one and I cut it up. I-- somebody always had a pair of scissors. So I cut out mittens out of that second jacket and doubled it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody had a-- made a needle out of wood on these-- you know, these three-tier bunk beds, cut off an eye, stitched this together. And when we saw him the next time, he couldn't believe it. I gave him a pair of mittens. But after one day of carrying stones, they were torn on one side. And then he had turned them around. He had another day out of that. But it made me feel so good. So I didn't have a-- a proper jacket. Anyway, the children were put into a separate compound for the day, during the day. And we were called upon-- it was you know, by word of mouth-- to volunteer to look after them, to occupy them during the day, and to play games with them, or whatever. And, of course, I volunteered. And I knew fully well what that meant, because the stories that came to us was that the schools that arrived, that groups of children arrived, their counselors went with them in order to keep them calm and organized. The-- whoever took care of the children ended up going into the gas with them. But this was your duty. And you had to look after the children. And this is what I did every day. And when-- when the visitors came, the food was better and more of it. And the children always got a little extra something, pudding. And I-- I had some pudding there, and then my portion I took and-- I took my life into my hands to go on off hours. And I sneaked it into my father's barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he got it. He told me, how can you do that? You're a child. You have to eat. You-- I said this was my second portion, and I didn't-- couldn't eat it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew all kind of people in his group, that two of them were of my group originally from Prague. And I-- they looked out for him a little bit, because you had shoes which somebody took from you. And he wouldn't take anybody else's shoes. So he was going to have a clear conscience at all times.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to you know, make a contact there when I could. And he-- there were wooden shoes that everybody wore, which was good in a way. They keep you warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: These little things you get. And these are your cushion. And I've-- there was something in Theresienstadt, but let's not go back to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you can. It's OK. We can go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- a-- a newly married couple that arrived in Theresienstadt. And she ended up sleeping next to me. They were totally assimilated. They didn't know anything. They came from some small place. And she asked me once how old I was. And I told her. And she said, well, I know the facts of life. And I said, well, I know. I know pretty well, but my parents don't

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12, 13. She-- she explained to me that her-- her husband is a doctor and that she's been married only six or seven days. And that seeing that we sleep next to each other, and because I had different hours-- I was working in a different place in the vegetable garden-- so she says, I'm the only one there, and if I would give her the opportunity to have a little privacy if it could be arranged. And if she gets caught, they wouldn't blame me for it. And she-- I said of course I would. And I did. And she said she'd never forget it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after that, my father had the-- had a flu. And he was very slow in recuperating. He-- he got over it, but he was very weak. And I was concerned about it. So I told this girl. I said, you know, this is the time you can do what you said you would do. So she brought me glucose through her husband. I don't know how he got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day, I'd bring my father this glucose. And he'd drink it straight, break the bottle and drink it. And it really made a tremendous difference. And that's another little cushion. And I remember her well, because unfortunately, she became pregnant. And when it was obvious that she was expecting a baby, she disappeared. So it was-- it was her wish to have that privacy. But it didn't work out too well. Anyway, we're back in Auschwitz. And time went on. The group became a little small as time went on. We-- people became sick, or there was a detail where they needed certain expertise and people, thinking that they will improve their lot, volunteered for it. And we don't know what happened to them after that. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] In 1943, we were told one day that the camp in Auschwitz, our show camp, would be dissolved. And that-- as they usually put on stories, we didn't believe one word of what it said to you. Everybody will be sent to work. And we need people in Germany. And you will be sent out in groups. You will no longer be together. And don't worry about it. You're all healthy people. And we're going to put you to good use. And later that day, I went as usual to be with my children. They were beautiful children. And we were about four counselors in this huge room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these children who didn't have families? Or the families were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had families. Their families were there. And the parents saw the children every day. They were really-- some of them stand out. I can never forget what they looked like. And one in particular. And that's one of my miracles, because it didn't make sense for me to survive, because I was working with the children. And obviously, we all knew that when the-- when the camp is dissolved, this is the end of our children. And so parents had to make up their minds whether they were going to stay behind or leave. There was one couple that I remember particularly. There little girl was so gorgeous. She was four years old. Her name was Eva. I don't know how they came to our group, because they were not Czech. They were from Austria. Maybe she was Czech. I don't know. And the mother's name was Gerta. And I don't remember a lot of things from last week, but I remember them. And his name was Heinz. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] She-- OK. They were just such dedicated parents. This was their first child. And when this day arrived, she was visiting the child in the-- in the playroom there. And he followed her. That's the first time I met him. And because that hour we had, we saw our own men. And he-- and this was in front of all of us. He said, Gerta, you have to go on the other side. You have to leave. It's our beautiful Eva, but you're not going to help her if you go with her. And we'll meet again after the war. We'll have more children. We have to survive. We're so young, he said. We're going to have beautiful children. And she couldn't. And he left, and he came back and he-- same story again, pleaded with her, cried. And she said, OK. I promise. I'm going to go out. And we'll meet after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were keeping the kids busy. And I don't know whether your senses are dulled, or whether you just say let's get it over with. You know? It's-- it's useless anyway. And I was standing on-- at my post there and answering the children, taking them to the bathroom, some of them, and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there comes Gerta rushing out, that mother of that little girl Eva. And she said, Heinz doesn't know about this. But I-- I have-- I can't live like that. And I may-- I am coming back. I'm going to be here. And I'm going to go with my Eva. And I said, you can't meant this. You promised your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you do this? And she said, I don't know where to find my husband. But this is final. This is final. So my three friends in the room started yelling Malka, you leave! You go! You don't need two in that corner. If she is really going to stay, you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I can't go. This is my job. She may not do it. She may fall apart. And no, no, there was a chorus going-- go, go, go, go! And I was worried about the chorus and attracting attention to it. So I-- so I went, and I started walking. I couldn't believe I was walking away from there. Re-joined my mother and sister. They thought a ghost was coming. They-- they couldn't believe that I-- I was there. And you know, there I was. And we said we have to go say goodbye to Papa. We have to go say goodbye to our father. And he [NON-ENGLISH] us. He blessed us. And-- And mother said to him to try [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] to stay with the living-- among the living. He was 49 years old. And he bent down to me. And he said, don't forget who you are. I never did. But we went outside. And we were put in-- in one-- somebody told me, it never occurred to me not to let on that we're related, because the Germans would separate us. There were other mothers and daughters. We are not to let on that we're-- that we're related. The Germans are getting very mean. And they would do that to spite us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went into one of these cattle wagons. And my mother said, don't believe them. They're not sending us out anywhere. They're going to un-hook this car, and they're going to take us back to the oven as soon as the gas is-- is-- is functional there. This is not a train anywhere. She says, have you ever seen a train where you go in cattle cars? If you're going out of Auschwitz, it's-- it's not-- don't put up your hopes.

speaker: interviewee
text: If we move out of here, and it'll-- an hour will pass, I will say yes, we're out of Auschwitz. And when we did leave, and the-- and the train kept moving, it was like something lifted off you, that-- that you never knew you were carrying. You just kept on going. And we did leave Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- I made myself notes because I didn't want to get--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we-- I-- I should mention that before we left Auschwitz, about the week before, two people broke out and made it to freedom. They were Czech, two Czech men.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your-- from you group? From your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not from our, they were from the next group of people. And we stood appell, that was the pattern that you stood in-- in formation. And they counted you endlessly, day in, day out, kept on counting you. And by the way, when they were counting us, on arrival-- I'm going back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans, they have to have order. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So this SS man noticed that I didn't have a jacket. It was very cold outside. And he demanded of the [SPEAKING GERMAN], she was the head of that room, why doesn't this little one have a-- I was still a little one, I guess-- have a jacket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she asked me, why don't you have a jacket? And I wasn't going to say that I cut it up. So I said this is my jacket, whatever I was wearing. So he got very angry at her. And he told her to go in and get one of her own jackets to give me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was, of course, a-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was a luscious jacket that I had for a day until they assigned a jacket to me. I wasn't going to say I cut it up. And no one was going to tell on me. I knew that. And there were other things when you stood in-- in appel, in formation. You-- my mother kept telling me to not to jump, to hop around [INAUDIBLE]. Because you're a child. It's sort of-- you can't put them down. You can't hold you down that much. But some day I wanted to skip around a little bit. And my sense of humor always took over, which probably saved my life in a way. And I was making jokes, made the others laugh. And she didn't like that at all. She says, I know. I know. I understand. I-- I really know what you're going through. I just don't want you to be noticed. I wore glasses. I'd just gotten my glasses. I was so proud of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because once I was skipping around there, this SS man said, why is she skipping around [SPEAKING GERMAN]? That [NON-ENGLISH], why is she all over the place? And-- and he looked at me. So my mother was worried he'd notice me the next day. So she took my glasses and threw them into the bushes. And I managed very well without them. They were not strong prescription. But this was such a-- each time such a sobering experience. OK, we have to cut now for a minute.
